https://gamma-cat.readthedocs.io/contribute/input.html

One can already add the data here with this snippet

```python
from astroquery.vizier import Vizier
vizier = Vizier()
lhaaso_cat = vizier.get_catalogs('J/ApJS/271/25/catalog')
table = lhaaso_cat.values()[0]
table.write("LHAASO_1st_catalog.ecsv", format="ascii.ecsv")
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/gammapy/gamma-cat/issues/248/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/gammapy/gamma-cat/issues/248/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

After creating a SpectrumDataset instance if one does by mistake 

`spectrum_dataset.model = bla `

instead of 

`spectrum_dataset.models = bla` 

_gammapy_ does not issue any error, instead lets the user go on with the analysis and obtain incorrect results.

(yes, there is the documentation, but in this case the absence of an error message does not help the user pinpoint _where_ to go in the docs to check what's wrong)

**Describe the solution you'd like**

There should be a check (throughout all _gammapy_, not only this example case) where if a user tries to set or get a non-existent class attribute a validation error should be raised.

I see that _gammapy_ uses _pydantic_ here and there, so I guess this could be the easiest solution, but I never used it myself.

version**


1.2

**Bug description**


Use of ObservationEventSampler fails because it's attempting to get the maximum value from the class, not from its data

**Expected behavior**

According to the source code it should be compatible with observations providing RadMax2D (otherwise there would be no reason to look for a maximum value)

**To Reproduce**

```python
from gammapy.data import ObservationsEventsSampler
observation = # any pointlike observation from a data store
model = # any sky model
obs_evt_sampler.simulate_observation(observation, models=Models(models=model))
```

1.2

**Bug description**


Use of ObservationEventSampler fails because it's attempting to get the maximum value from the class, not from its data

**Expected behavior**

According to the source code it should be compatible with observations providing RadMax2D (otherwise there would be no reason to look for a maximum value)

from gammapy.data import ObservationsEventsSampler
observation = # any pointlike observation from a data store
model = # any sky model

`etrue = observation.psf.axes[0].edges` is too long when getting the energy resolution should be `center`

like this the following example works,

```python
from gammapy.data import Observation, ObservationsEventsSampler
from gammapy.modeling.models import Models

obs_evt_sampler = ObservationsEventsSampler(outdir=None)

model = SkyModel(spectral_model=spectral_model, spatial_model=spatial_model, name="target_source")

# from a real observation
data_store = DataStore.from_dir("$GAMMAPY_DATA/cta-1dc/index/gps")
real_observation = data_store.obs(int(observations.ids[0]))

# sky model of a Crab-like source at a convenient celestial coordinate
spectral_model = LogParabolaSpectralModel(
    amplitude=3.39e-11 * u.Unit("cm-2 s-1 TeV-1"),
    alpha=2.51,
    beta=0.21,
    reference=1 * u.TeV,
)

spatial_model = PointSpatialModel(
    lon_0=real_observation.pointing.get_icrs().ra,
    lat_0=real_observation.pointing.get_icrs().dec,
    frame="icrs"
)

fake_obs = obs_evt_sampler.simulate_observation(real_observation, models=Models(models=model))

# from an originally fake observation with some dummy (full-enclosure) IRFs
irfs = {"aeff": some_observation.aeff, "edisp": some_observation.edisp, "psf":some_observation.psf, "bkg":some_observation.bkg}

fake_observation =  Observation.create(
    pointing=some_pointing,
    livetime=some_time,
    irfs=irfs,
    location=a_location,
)

fake_obs = obs_evt_sampler.simulate_observation(fake_obs, models=Models(models=model))
```

in both cases `test_observation.events` will contain the sampled events.

The code above might be a new tutorial for the use of this class from full-enclosure IRFs, but the limitation for point-like IRFs still stands.

An alternative might be to simulate from full-enclosure and then analyze as point-like, but I am not sure how to do it making sure that the final estimated statistics is correct","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/gammapy/gammapy/issues/comments/2460035678/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

**Description**

This pull request fixes two small bugs that are preventing `gammapy.data.ObservationsEventsSampler` to work on full-enclosure-based observations (for the point-like case see #5544 ).

I can add a new tutorial with a short snippet on how to use this class (either as a separate PR after this fix PR is merged or directly here if preferred by the maintainers).

Regarding the testing (to be integrated):
- the fix for `dataset_kwargs` is needed because in the test this is always defined, never `None`
- the energy axes fix is needed because of the fix above (if the axis is None when `create_map_dataset_from_observation` is called then the (faulty) function `_default_energy_axis` is called

Closes #5542.

<!-- Don't be shy! Github issues or pull requests are welcome any time. -->
<!-- But if you first want to chat, use the Gammapy Slack. -->
<!-- See https://gammapy.org/contact.html -->

**Description**

This pull request fixes two small bugs that are preventing `gammapy.data.ObservationsEventsSampler` to work on full-enclosure-based observations (for the point-like case see #5544 ).

I can add a new tutorial with a short snippet on how to use this class (either as a separate PR after this fix PR is merged or directly here if preferred by the maintainers).

Regarding the testing (to be integrated):
- the fix for `dataset_kwargs` is needed because in the test this is always defined, never `None`
- the energy axes fix is needed because of the fix above (if the axis is None when `create_map_dataset_from_observation` is called then the (faulty) function `_default_energy_axis` is called

Closes #5542.

version**

1.2

**Bug description**

specifying a PointSpatialModel while using CTA IRFs loaded with `load_irf_dict_from_file` results in nan energy flux

**Expected behavior**

Since the same works for real observations from a data store I would expect the same behaviour

**To Reproduce**

building on the 1D spectrum simulation:

```python
import astropy.units as u
import numpy as np

from astropy.coordinates import SkyCoord, Angle
from gammapy.data import FixedPointingInfo, Observation, observatory_locations
from gammapy.irf import load_irf_dict_from_file
from gammapy.datasets import SpectrumDataset, SpectrumDatasetOnOff
from gammapy.estimators import FluxPointsEstimator
from gammapy.maps import MapAxis, RegionGeom
from gammapy.makers import SpectrumDatasetMaker
from gammapy.modeling.models import (
    LogParabolaSpectralModel,
    PointSpatialModel,
    SkyModel,
)
from regions import CircleSkyRegion


observation_livetime = 50 * u.h


offset = 0.4 * u.deg

pointing_position = SkyCoord(0, 0, unit="deg", frame="galactic")

pointing = FixedPointingInfo(
    fixed_icrs=pointing_position.icrs,
)


energy_axis = MapAxis.from_edges(
    np.logspace(-0.5, 1.0, 10), unit="TeV", name="energy", interp="log"
)
energy_axis_true = MapAxis.from_edges(
    np.logspace(-1.2, 2.0, 31), unit="TeV", name="energy_true", interp="log"
)

on_region_radius = Angle("0.11 deg")

center = pointing_position.directional_offset_by(
    position_angle=0 * u.deg, separation=offset
)
on_region = CircleSkyRegion(center=center, radius=on_region_radius)


irfs = load_irf_dict_from_file(
    "/Users/michele/Software/gammapy/gammapy-datasets/dev/cta-1dc/caldb/data/cta/1dc/bcf/South_z20_50h/irf_file.fits"
)

location = observatory_locations["cta_south"]

simulated_observation = Observation.create(
    pointing=pointing,
    livetime=observation_livetime,
    irfs=irfs,
    location=location,
)

spectral_model = LogParabolaSpectralModel(
    amplitude=3.39e-11 * u.Unit("cm-2 s-1 TeV-1"),
    alpha=2.51,
    beta=0.21,
    reference=1 * u.TeV,
)



spatial_model = PointSpatialModel(
    lon_0=pointing_position.spherical.lon,
    lat_0=pointing_position.spherical.lat,
    frame=pointing_position.frame.name,
)

model = SkyModel(
    spectral_model=spectral_model, 
    spatial_model=spatial_model,      <----------- if this line is commented out it works
    name="target_source"
)


on_region_geometry = RegionGeom.create(region=on_region, axes=[energy_axis])

empty_spectrum_dataset = SpectrumDataset.create(
    geom=on_region_geometry,
    energy_axis_true=energy_axis_true,
    name="simulated-observation-0",
)
spectrum_dataset_maker = SpectrumDatasetMaker(
    selection=["exposure", "edisp", "background"]
)

spectrum_dataset = spectrum_dataset_maker.run(
    empty_spectrum_dataset, simulated_observation
)


spectrum_dataset.models = model
spectrum_dataset.fake()


spectrum_dataset_on_off = SpectrumDatasetOnOff.from_spectrum_dataset(
    dataset=spectrum_dataset, acceptance=1, acceptance_off=3
)
spectrum_dataset_on_off.fake(npred_background=spectrum_dataset.npred_background())


flux_points_estimator = FluxPointsEstimator(
    source="target_source", energy_edges=energy_axis.edges, selection_optional="all"
)

flux_points = flux_points_estimator.run(spectrum_dataset_on_off)


flux_points_table = flux_points.to_table(
    sed_type="e2dnde", format="gadf-sed", formatted=True
)
flux_points_table
```

    spectral_model=spectral_model, 
    spatial_model=spatial_model,      <----------- if this line is commented out it works
    name="target_source"
)


on_region_geometry = RegionGeom.create(region=on_region, axes=[energy_axis])

empty_spectrum_dataset = SpectrumDataset.create(
    geom=on_region_geometry,
    energy_axis_true=energy_axis_true,
    name="simulated-observation-0",
)
spectrum_dataset_maker = SpectrumDatasetMaker(
    selection=["exposure", "edisp", "background"]
)

spectrum_dataset = spectrum_dataset_maker.run(
    empty_spectrum_dataset, simulated_observation
)


spectrum_dataset.models = model
spectrum_dataset.fake()


spectrum_dataset_on_off = SpectrumDatasetOnOff.from_spectrum_dataset(
    dataset=spectrum_dataset, acceptance=1, acceptance_off=3
)
spectrum_dataset_on_off.fake(npred_background=spectrum_dataset.npred_background())


flux_points_estimator = FluxPointsEstimator(
    source="target_source", energy_edges=energy_axis.edges, selection_optional="all"
)

flux_points = flux_points_estimator.run(spectrum_dataset_on_off)


flux_points_table = flux_points.to_table(
    sed_type="e2dnde", format="gadf-sed", formatted=True
)
flux_points_table
```

the loading of sample datasets from yt itself.

It might also be a shortcoming in the documentation in this or the other project.

Feel free to move the issue there if this is not the case.

**Code for reproduction**

```python
import yt

import yt_idv

ds = yt.load_sample("IsolatedGalaxy")

rc = yt_idv.render_context(height=800, width=800, gui=True)
sg = rc.add_scene(ds, "density", no_ghost=True)
rc.run()
```

**Actual outcome**

```
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'pooch'

If the problem persists, please file an issue at https://github.com/yt-project/yt/issues/new
```

and then in series the same for `pandas` and `h5py` respectively.

**Expected outcome**

The example should work immediately (and it does, when those 3 dependencies are installed).

**Version Information**

  * Operating System: macos 14.7
  * Python Version: 3.12.7
  * yt version: 4.3.1 via conda
  * Other Libraries (if applicable): yt-idv 0.4.1 via PyPI

version fails to be fetched from editable installs","user":{"login":"HealthyPear","id":17836610,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3ODM2NjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17836610?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear","html_url":"https://github.com/HealthyPear","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":39740769,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzOTc0MDc2OQ==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/gammapy/gammapy/labels/bug","name":"bug","color":"fc2929","default":true,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-10-25T10:01:03Z","updated_at":"2024-10-25T10:01:03Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"**Gammapy version**
I am using the latest development version installed in editable mode (i.e. `pip install -e $SOURCECODEPATH`).

**Bug description**

outside of the source code folder any code crashes as soon as gammapy tries to know its version

```python
In [1]: import gammapy

In [2]: gammapy.__version__
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
AttributeError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[2], line 1
----> 1 gammapy.__version__

AttributeError: module 'gammapy' has no attribute '__version__'
```

works only in the source code folder and the home folder.

**Expected behavior**

A developer should be able to use `gammapy` from any path.

**To Reproduce**

E.g. `from gammapy.irf import load_irf_dict_from_file`

**Other information**

From your `__init__.py` Gammapy is trying to read version information from the installed package metadata, which editable installs sometimes don’t handle as expected (see e.g. [setuptools docs](https://setuptools.pypa.io/en/latest/userguide/development_mode.html#limitations)).

I am guessing the version is managed by git tags, so you could benefit from using instead [setuptools-scm](https://github.com/pypa/setuptools-scm) to auto-generate the version file when someone installs gammapy with  `pip install -e`. This way you can provide the package version dynamically without relying only on the package metadata.

For an example see [ctapipe](https://github.com/cta-observatory/ctapipe/tree/main).

version**

1.2

**Bug description**

When writing a `FluxPoints` table (_.ecsv_) to file it is not possible to overwrite it.

**Expected behavior**

The `overwrite` argument should be taken into account.

**To Reproduce**

From any analysis get a `FluxPoints` instance (in my case it was from obtained from `LightCurveEstimator.run()`)
and call the `write()` method with `overwrite=True`.

You will get the following error,

`OSError: File xxxxxx.ecsv already exists. If you mean to replace it then use the argument "overwrite=True".`

**Other information**

The bug is here: https://github.com/gammapy/gammapy/blob/2475a006e02ac1497eccc362de61e68a5f7a10eb/gammapy/estimators/points/core.py#L270

It should be sufficient to pass the `overwrite` argument to `table.write()`, because the default (inherited from `astropy.table.Table.write()`) is `False`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/gammapy/gammapy/issues/5253/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/gammapy/gammapy/issues/5253/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

https://docs.gammapy.org/1.2/tutorials/starting/analysis_1.html

and I had to go into the source code to know e.g. which are the allowed string identifiers for the
`background` subsection.

For example in this case, going backwards from the class I was using, I traced back the answer to my doubt:

`AnalysisConfig` --> `BackgroundConfig` --> `BackgroundMethodEnum`

which allowed me to understand the possible choices are `reflected`, `fov_background` or `ring`.

Apologies if I have overlooked a clearer source of information, but if I am right, then at least the API reference should be updated with more classes (to start, all those used by `AnalysisConfig` in order to make clear which are all the supported options to use when filling an analysis configuration file).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/gammapy/gammapy/issues/5181/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/gammapy/gammapy/issues/5181/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

gammapy v1.2

**Bug description**
A short description what the bug is (usually 1-2 sentences)

Using the high-level interface one has to specificy a radius for the ON region as there doesn't seem any default value as the source code suggests (because there is no clear documentation on the available options for this interface - see  #5181)

the class/option hierarchy is `AnalysisConfig` ---> `DatasetsConfig` ---> `on_region=SpatialCircleConfig(frame=None, lon=None, lat=None, radius=None)`

**Expected behavior**
A clear and concise description of what you expected to happen.

Either set a default or change the class attribute to not be optional

```python
class SpatialCircleConfig(GammapyBaseConfig):
    frame: Optional[FrameEnum] = None
    lon: Optional[AngleType] = None
    lat: Optional[AngleType] = None
    radius: Optional[AngleType] = None 
```

**To Reproduce**
Steps to reproduce the behavior.
See https://matthewrocklin.com/blog/work/2018/02/28/minimal-bug-reports

Run the high-level interface tutorial (https://docs.gammapy.org/1.2/tutorials/starting/analysis_1.html) without specifying the ON region radius (which weirdly is not specified even there) and you when you try to get the datasets from the Analysis instance you will get

your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**
Currently the `SensitivityEstimator`  allows to perform sensitivity estimation neither of cube datasets nor of joint datasets (see e.g. event types). It currently relies on the `CountsStatistic` classes to compute the expected `n_signal` to reach a given significance which limits its scope. Another approach is required to handle those more complex cases. 

**Describe the solution you'd like**
It could rely on computing the TS distribution in the non-null hypothesis using the log likelihood of the Asimov dataset (i.e. a dataset with counts=npred)  and the non central chi2 distribution. Once the TS distribution under H1 (source strength>0) is known, one can compute the required source strength (norm) to have 50% of measurements above a given significance threshold. The underlying statistic is detailed in Cowan et al (2011): https://arxiv.org/abs/1007.1727 

I have prepared a small notebook to check that it can work:
https://gist.github.com/registerrier/fad2c4559f6c1758a510095c5e63f9cf

**Additional context**
This would solve some of the issues raised #4576 (.i.e. no simulations needed). This could also offer a means to compute sensitivity in flux points estimation in a similar manner to what #4584 is proposing.

Opinions @adonath @bkhelifi @QRemy @AtreyeeS ?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/gammapy/gammapy/issues/4712/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":1},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/gammapy/gammapy/issues/4712/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/gammapy/gammapy/issues/comments/1943944774","html_url":"https://github.com/gammapy/gammapy/issues/4712#issuecomment-1943944774","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/gammapy/gammapy/issues/4712","id":1943944774,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAJm2KM5z3j5G","user":{"login":"HealthyPear","id":17836610,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3ODM2NjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17836610?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear","html_url":"https://github.com/HealthyPear","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-02-14T14:42:45Z","updated_at":"2024-02-14T14:42:45Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"Sorry coming back to this after a while....

#### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.
I'd like to use hvplot (but more generally the whole holoviz ecosystem) on data structures defined by the
astropy project.
Such data structures can be simple tables, but also Quantity Tables with units (see [their docs](https://docs.astropy.org/en/stable/table/index.html)).

#### Describe the solution you'd like
Essentially an analogue of what is already available for other libraries like Pandas

#### Describe alternatives you've considered
Astropy can export its tables into a pandas dataframe, but in doing so you lose the units metadata.

your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**
I might have overlooked this in the documentation, but there is no API member that allows to convert
a background map containing a counts/(energy steradian) to a map of counts

**Describe the solution you'd like**
What I have is a simple function that eats a `WcsNDMap`, interpolates on some geometry with reconstructed energy axis and multiplies by the solid angle of said geometry

my use-case might be very specific, I am not an expert of gammapy but if you want I can already open a PR with this small function

s{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adonath/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adonath/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adonath/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adonath/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adonath/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/adonath/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"open_issues":97,"closed_issues":121,"state":"open","created_at":"2022-09-23T16:20:19Z","updated_at":"2023-11-02T22:25:07Z","due_on":"2023-11-24T08:00:00Z","closed_at":null},"comments":1,"created_at":"2023-08-02T17:20:11Z","updated_at":"2023-11-03T09:44:25Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**
Currently the `SensitivityEstimator`  allows to perform sensitivity estimation neither of cube datasets nor of joint datasets (see e.g. event types). It currently relies on the `CountsStatistic` classes to compute the expected `n_signal` to reach a given significance which limits its scope. Another approach is required to handle those more complex cases. 

**Describe the solution you'd like**
It could rely on computing the TS distribution in the non-null hypothesis using the log likelihood of the Asimov dataset (i.e. a dataset with counts=npred)  and the non central chi2 distribution. Once the TS distribution under H1 (source strength>0) is known, one can compute the required source strength (norm) to have 50% of measurements above a given significance threshold. The underlying statistic is detailed in Cowan et al (2011): https://arxiv.org/abs/1007.1727 

I have prepared a small notebook to check that it can work:
https://gist.github.com/registerrier/fad2c4559f6c1758a510095c5e63f9cf

**Additional context**
This would solve some of the issues raised #4576 (.i.e. no simulations needed). This could also offer a means to compute sensitivity in flux points estimation in a similar manner to what #4584 is proposing.

Opinions @adonath @bkhelifi @QRemy @AtreyeeS ?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/gammapy/gammapy/issues/4712/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":1},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/gammapy/gammapy/issues/4712/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/gammapy/gammapy/issues/comments/1792134314","html_url":"https://github.com/gammapy/gammapy/issues/4712#issuecomment-1792134314","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/gammapy/gammapy/issues/4712","id":1792134314,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAJm2KM5q0cyq","user":{"login":"HealthyPear","id":17836610,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3ODM2NjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17836610?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear","html_url":"https://github.com/HealthyPear","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-11-03T09:44:25Z","updated_at":"2023-11-03T09:44:25Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"Hi @registerrier ,

as I mentioned to you this approach is working quite well for SWGO after I adapted it to our code base.

We will now add the experimental implementation to our data processing framework (unfortunately still not public, but hopefully soon).

Do you think we should discuss how to incorporate it here?

I guess the minimum would be:
- the function to compute the cash statistics (which is the one used by the map datasets)
- the class for the TS defined by Cowan
- a new MapSensitivityEstimator which I guess can be the one we are using but further improved (what is currently missing e.g. is the possibility to define a minimum number of excess gamma rays and add the significance criteria to show where this is dominated by excess or background systematics; the former is more negligible for instruments like SWGO than CTA, but I think it should be added also)

What do you think?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/gammapy/gammapy/issues/comments/1792134314/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

```python
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
TypeError                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In [147], line 1
----> 1 plot_sky(FixedTarget(coord=source_altaz, name=source_name), Observer.at_site('Roque de los Muchachos'), time, north_to_east_ccw=False)
      3 plt.legend(loc='center left', bbox_to_anchor=(1.25, 0.5))
      4 plt.show()

File ~/Applications/mambaforge/envs/astro/lib/python3.10/site-packages/astropy/units/decorators.py:316, in QuantityInput.__call__.<locals>.wrapper(*func_args, **func_kwargs)
    314 # Call the original function with any equivalencies in force.
    315 with add_enabled_equivalencies(self.equivalencies):
--> 316     return_ = wrapped_function(*func_args, **func_kwargs)
    318 # Return
    319 ra = wrapped_signature.return_annotation

File ~/Applications/mambaforge/envs/astro/lib/python3.10/site-packages/astroplan/plots/sky.py:120, in plot_sky(target, observer, time, ax, style_kwargs, north_to_east_ccw, grid, az_label_offset, warn_below_horizon, style_sheet)

    121 if style_kwargs is None:
    122     style_kwargs = {}

TypeError: gca() got an unexpected keyword argument 'projection'
```

caused by the deprecation and later removal of the keywords associated to plt.cga()

see https://matplotlib.org/stable/api/prev_api_changes/api_changes_3.4.0.html#pyplot-gca-and-figure-gca-keyword-arguments

The deprecation notice using matplotlib v3.4 read in fact as follows,

> Calling gca() with keyword arguments was deprecated in Matplotlib 3.4. 
> Starting two minor releases later, gca() will take no keyword arguments.  
> The gca() function should only be used to get the current axes,
> or if no axes exist, create new axes with default keyword arguments.
> To create a new axes with non-default arguments,
> use plt.axes() or plt.subplot().

So solution to this problem is to:
1. require a matpltlib version way more recent than the one you are currently using as the bottom limit in your `setup.cfg` (v1.4)
2. modifying the `plot_sky` function to do `ax = fig.add_subplot(projection='polar')` instead of `ax = plt.gca(projection='polar')`","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astroplan/issues/561/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astroplan/issues/561/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"}}

What is the problem this feature will solve?

I could not find a function in astropy to obtain the steradians from an opening angle, and since [in pyirf we defined it ourselves](https://github.com/cta-observatory/pyirf/blob/main/pyirf/utils.py), I thought it might be nice to make this (I think common) feature available from a more extensive project that we also use anyway.

I could not find this quickly, so I apologize for overlooking it.

In case this might be of interest to you I am willing to open a PR, just tell me where this function could be stored. I was thinking as a new method of the Angle class, but since it can also work with quantities, maybe it can be put in a more high-level place.

### Describe the desired outcome

Either `cone_solid_angle(5*u.deg)` (and `cone_solid_angle(Angle(5,u.deg))` ) or 

```
a = 5*u.deg
a.to("sr")
```

and

```
a = Angle(5,u.deg)
a.to_solid_angle
```

> The gca() function should only be used to get the current axes,
> or if no axes exist, create new axes with default keyword arguments.
> To create a new axes with non-default arguments,
> use plt.axes() or plt.subplot().

1. require a matpltlib version way more recent than the one you are currently using as the bottom limit in your `setup.cfg` (v1.4)

Description

See title :)

### Expected behavior

Probably there is a good deep reason why this doesn't happen, but I would expect that a quantity initialized with an angle unit is effectively an angle. 

### How to Reproduce

```python
import astropy.units as u
from astropy.units import Quantity
from astropy.coordinates import Angle

rotation = 5 * u.deg
isinstance(rotation, Angle) # this returns False

angle = Angle("5d")
isinstance(angle, Quantity) # this returns True
```

<!-- We have several automatic features that run when a pull request is open.
They can appear daunting but do not worry because maintainers will help

### Description
<!-- Provide a general description of what your pull request does.
Complete the following sentence and add relevant details as you see fit. -->

When converting between coordinate frames what is currently happening is that `lon` and `lat` attributes get converted to `radians`, which depending on the current unit registry might or might not be defined as an alias to `rad`.

Luckily, the latter is always defined as the official name for radians, so it makes sense to make sure that we convert these quantities always using the guaranteed unit name.

Fixes #14580.

Note that #14933 was originally opened to update the documentation and recall the user to use a context manager when enabling e.g. CDS units (which do not define "radians", only "rad"). This PR should be the effective fix, but I think it still makes sense to have also the older PR.

<!-- In addition please ensure that the pull request title is descriptive
and allows maintainers to infer the applicable subpackage(s). -->

### Description
<!-- Provide a general description of what your pull request does.
Complete the following sentence and add relevant details as you see fit. -->

When converting between coordinate frames what is currently happening is that `lon` and `lat` attributes get converted to `radians`, which depending on the current unit registry might or might not be defined as an alias to `rad`.

Luckily, the latter is always defined as the official name for radians, so it makes sense to make sure that we convert these quantities always using the guaranteed unit name.

Fixes #14580.

Note that #14933 was originally opened to update the documentation and recall the user to use a context manager when enabling e.g. CDS units (which do not define "radians", only "rad"). This PR should be the effective fix, but I think it still makes sense to have also the older PR.

<!-- In addition please ensure that the pull request title is descriptive
and allows maintainers to infer the applicable subpackage(s). -->

They can appear daunting but do not worry because maintainers will help

### Description
<!-- Provide a general description of what your pull request does.
Complete the following sentence and add relevant details as you see fit. -->

When converting between coordinate frames what is currently happening is that `lon` and `lat` attributes get converted to `radians`, which depending on the current unit registry might or might not be defined as an alias to `rad`.

Luckily, the latter is always defined as the official name for radians, so it makes sense to make sure that we convert these quantities always using the guaranteed unit name.

Fixes #14580.

Note that #14933 was originally opened to update the documentation and recall the user to use a context manager when enabling e.g. CDS units (which do not define "radians", only "rad"). This PR should be the effective fix, but I think it still makes sense to have also the older PR.

<!-- In addition please ensure that the pull request title is descriptive
and allows maintainers to infer the applicable subpackage(s). -->

### Description
<!-- Provide a general description of what your pull request does.
Complete the following sentence and add relevant details as you see fit. -->

When converting between coordinate frames what is currently happening is that `lon` and `lat` attributes get converted to `radians`, which depending on the current unit registry might or might not be defined as an alias to `rad`.

Luckily, the latter is always defined as the official name for radians, so it makes sense to make sure that we convert these quantities always using the guaranteed unit name.

Fixes #14580.

Note that #14933 was originally opened to update the documentation and recall the user to use a context manager when enabling e.g. CDS units (which do not define "radians", only "rad"). This PR should be the effective fix, but I think it still makes sense to have also the older PR.

<!-- In addition please ensure that the pull request title is descriptive
and allows maintainers to infer the applicable subpackage(s). -->

They can appear daunting but do not worry because maintainers will help

### Description
<!-- Provide a general description of what your pull request does.
Complete the following sentence and add relevant details as you see fit. -->

When converting between coordinate frames what is currently happening is that `lon` and `lat` attributes get converted to `radians`, which depending on the current unit registry might or might not be defined as an alias to `rad`.

Luckily, the latter is always defined as the official name for radians, so it makes sense to make sure that we convert these quantities always using the guaranteed unit name.

Fixes #14580.

Note that #14933 was originally opened to update the documentation and recall the user to use a context manager when enabling e.g. CDS units (which do not define "radians", only "rad"). This PR should be the effective fix, but I think it still makes sense to have also the older PR.

<!-- In addition please ensure that the pull request title is descriptive
and allows maintainers to infer the applicable subpackage(s). -->

### Description
<!-- Provide a general description of what your pull request does.
Complete the following sentence and add relevant details as you see fit. -->

When converting between coordinate frames what is currently happening is that `lon` and `lat` attributes get converted to `radians`, which depending on the current unit registry might or might not be defined as an alias to `rad`.

Luckily, the latter is always defined as the official name for radians, so it makes sense to make sure that we convert these quantities always using the guaranteed unit name.

Fixes #14580.

Note that #14933 was originally opened to update the documentation and recall the user to use a context manager when enabling e.g. CDS units (which do not define "radians", only "rad"). This PR should be the effective fix, but I think it still makes sense to have also the older PR.

<!-- In addition please ensure that the pull request title is descriptive
and allows maintainers to infer the applicable subpackage(s). -->

<!-- We have several automatic features that run when a pull request is open.
They can appear daunting but do not worry because maintainers will help

### Description
<!-- Provide a general description of what your pull request does.
Complete the following sentence and add relevant details as you see fit. -->

When converting between coordinate frames what is currently happening is that `lon` and `lat` attributes get converted to `radians`, which depending on the current unit registry might or might not be defined as an alias to `rad`.

Luckily, the latter is always defined as the official name for radians, so it makes sense to make sure that we convert these quantities always using the guaranteed unit name.

Fixes #14580.

Note that #14933 was originally opened to update the documentation and recall the user to use a context manager when enabling e.g. CDS units (which do not define "radians", only "rad"). This PR should be the effective fix, but I think it still makes sense to have also the older PR.

<!-- In addition please ensure that the pull request title is descriptive
and allows maintainers to infer the applicable subpackage(s). -->

the following line. -->

### Description
<!-- Provide a general description of what your pull request does.
Complete the following sentence and add relevant details as you see fit. -->

When converting between coordinate frames what is currently happening is that `lon` and `lat` attributes get converted to `radians`, which depending on the current unit registry might or might not be defined as an alias to `rad`.

Luckily, the latter is always defined as the official name for radians, so it makes sense to make sure that we convert these quantities always using the guaranteed unit name.

Fixes #14580.

Note that #14933 was originally opened to update the documentation and recall the user to use a context manager when enabling e.g. CDS units (which do not define "radians", only "rad"). This PR should be the effective fix, but I think it still makes sense to have also the older PR.

<!-- In addition please ensure that the pull request title is descriptive
and allows maintainers to infer the applicable subpackage(s). -->

### Description
<!-- Provide a general description of what your pull request does.
Complete the following sentence and add relevant details as you see fit. -->

When converting between coordinate frames what is currently happening is that `lon` and `lat` attributes get converted to `radians`, which depending on the current unit registry might or might not be defined as an alias to `rad`.

Luckily, the latter is always defined as the official name for radians, so it makes sense to make sure that we convert these quantities always using the guaranteed unit name.

Fixes #14580.

Note that #14933 was originally opened to update the documentation and recall the user to use a context manager when enabling e.g. CDS units (which do not define "radians", only "rad"). This PR should be the effective fix, but I think it still makes sense to have also the older PR.

<!-- In addition please ensure that the pull request title is descriptive
and allows maintainers to infer the applicable subpackage(s). -->

SOME_CONSTANT = 1

summary line.

some explanation.
"""

I get

```
GL01: Docstring text (summary) should start in the line immediately after the opening quotes (not in the same line, or leaving a blank line in between)

```

the same thing also in the case of a _single summary line_ (for which I would argue that these 2 warnings should be disabled by default as per PEP257), doesn't matter if the quotes are on the same line or not.

https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/issues/450

I've included information on setting up the pre-commit hook in the validation page in the docs.  If you want to test on a repo of your own before this is merged, you can add the following to your `.pre-commit-config.yaml` file:

```yaml
  - repo: https://github.com/stefmolin/numpydoc
    rev: <latest commit hash>
    hooks:
      - id: numpydoc-validation
```

The hook can also be run on the command line after you reinstall the package:

```shell
$ python -m numpydoc.hooks.validate_docstrings --help
```

Specific checks can be ignored with inline comments (`# numpydoc ignore=GL08`), or globally ignored by passing them in to the hook via a command line argument or a configuration file (`pyproject.toml` or `setup.cfg`). I've added options for regex to determine overrides for GL08 and SS05, which frequently come up in development; the former for inherited docstrings or dunder methods, and the latter for valid starting words like "Process" or "Access."

Is there any interest in running the hook on further development of numpydoc? I can set that up here as well, if desired.

https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/issues/450

I've included information on setting up the pre-commit hook in the validation page in the docs.  If you want to test on a repo of your own before this is merged, you can add the following to your `.pre-commit-config.yaml` file:

```yaml
  - repo: https://github.com/stefmolin/numpydoc
    rev: <latest commit hash>
    hooks:
      - id: numpydoc-validation
```

The hook can also be run on the command line after you reinstall the package:

```shell
$ python -m numpydoc.hooks.validate_docstrings --help
```

Specific checks can be ignored with inline comments (`# numpydoc ignore=GL08`), or globally ignored by passing them in to the hook via a command line argument or a configuration file (`pyproject.toml` or `setup.cfg`). I've added options for regex to determine overrides for GL08 and SS05, which frequently come up in development; the former for inherited docstrings or dunder methods, and the latter for valid starting words like "Process" or "Access."

Is there any interest in running the hook on further development of numpydoc? I can set that up here as well, if desired.

```
    """Bad, bad docstring!

    Parameters
    ---------
    x : `int`
    y:
        Another variable.

    Returns
    -------
    z : `int`
        The sum of ``x`` and ``y``.
    """
```

which contains many issues, but the issue of the shorter underline triggers a rather misterious
`PR01    | Parameters {'x', 'y'} not documented`.

In fact, after adding the missing dash I get the next issues, `PR01`, `PR02`, `PR07` and `PR10`.

From the [list of available validation checks](https://numpydoc.readthedocs.io/en/latest/validation.html) the shorter underline problem is not present, I guess because it's more an RsT issue, but since _numpydoc_ uses RsT (unless I overlooked this), I think it should be a proper validation check.

https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/issues/450

I've included information on setting up the pre-commit hook in the validation page in the docs.  If you want to test on a repo of your own before this is merged, you can add the following to your `.pre-commit-config.yaml` file:

```yaml
  - repo: https://github.com/stefmolin/numpydoc
    rev: <latest commit hash>
    hooks:
      - id: numpydoc-validation
```

The hook can also be run on the command line after you reinstall the package:

```shell
$ python -m numpydoc.hooks.validate_docstrings --help
```

Specific checks can be ignored with inline comments (`# numpydoc ignore=GL08`), or globally ignored by passing them in to the hook via a command line argument or a configuration file (`pyproject.toml` or `setup.cfg`). I've added options for regex to determine overrides for GL08 and SS05, which frequently come up in development; the former for inherited docstrings or dunder methods, and the latter for valid starting words like "Process" or "Access."

Is there any interest in running the hook on further development of numpydoc? I can set that up here as well, if desired.

+A safer way to use a CDS unit is to start a context manager and perform
+any operation related to these units inside of it, for example:
+
+    >>> with cds.enable():
+    >>>     MOMENTUM_UNIT = u.Unit("GeV/c")
+    >>> particle_momentum = 5 *     pco = erfa.s2c(srepr.lon.radian, srepr.lat.radian)

self = <Longitude 175. deg>, attr = 'radian'

    def __getattr__(self, attr):
        """
        Quantities are able to directly convert to other units that
        have the same physical type.
        """
        if not self._include_easy_conversion_members:
            raise AttributeError(
                f"'{self.__class__.__name__}' object has no '{attr}' member"
            )

        def get_virtual_unit_attribute():
            registry = get_current_unit_registry().registry
            to_unit = registry.get(attr, None)
            if to_unit is None:
                return None

            try:
                return self.unit.to(
                    to_unit, self.value, equivalencies=self.equivalencies
                )
            except UnitsError:

        if value is None:
>           raise AttributeError(

../../mambaforge/envs/swgo-plot/lib/python3.9/site-packages/astropy/units/quantity.py:1102: AttributeError
```

### Expected behavior

It should work the same in any environment if the package version is the same

### How to Reproduce

From a pdb session triggered by pytest I get that the triggering line is the following

```
-> lon[k] = sky_coords[k].geocentrictrueecliptic.lon.deg
(Pdb) k
0
(Pdb) sky_coords[k]
<SkyCoord (ICRS): (ra, dec) in deg
    (175., -0.5)>
```

indeed if (still inside pytest/pdb) I try to perform the transformation I get,

`(Pdb) sky_coords[k].geocentrictrueecliptic
*** AttributeError: Longitude instance has no attribute 'radian'`

To be sure I try to replicate the issue from scratch in a separate IPython session,

```ipython
In [1]: from astropy import units as u
   ...: from astropy.coordinates import SkyCoord
   ...: c = SkyCoord(ra=10.625*u.degree, dec=41.2*u.degree, frame='icrs')

In [2]: c.geocentrictrueecliptic
Out[2]:
<SkyCoord (GeocentricTrueEcliptic: equinox=J2000.000, obstime=J2000.000): (lon, lat, distance) in (deg, deg, )
    (27.76134106, 33.31188749, 1.)>

In [3]: c.geocentrictrueecliptic.lon.deg
Out[3]: 27.761341057229142
```

In the specific of this issue what I am suggesting is to modify the API related to this part of the frame conversion,

```
File ~/Applications/mambaforge/envs/pyswgo/lib/python3.11/site-packages/astropy/coordinates/builtin_frames/utils.py:297, in atciqz(srepr, astrom)
    294 ignore_distance = srepr_distance.unit == u.one
    296 # BCRS coordinate direction (unit vector).
--> 297 pco = erfa.s2c(srepr.lon.radian, srepr.lat.radian)
    299 # Find BCRS direction of Sun to object
    300 if ignore_distance:
    301     # No distance to object, assume a long way away
```

so instead of doing `srepr.lon.radian` either:
- always do `srepr.lon.rad`
- or permanently add `radian` to the container registry as an alias to `rad`

what do you think?

Since the installation requires the use of _conda_, you might as well instruct the user to set any environment variable using _conda_ itself for the environment specific to the _gammapy_ version in use, instead of using the shell which is global,

```
$ curl -O https://gammapy.org/download/install/gammapy-1.1-environment.yml
$ conda env create -f gammapy-1.1-environment.yml
$ conda activate gammapy-1.1
$ gammapy download notebooks
$ gammapy download datasets
$ conda env config vars set GAMMAPY_DATA=$PWD/gammapy-datasets/1.1

regarding the first point, me and a collaborator from SWGO are trying to get a sensitivity curve (as soon as he activates an account here I'll tag him).

We immediately got stuck because of a series of cascading problems originating from these issues:
- in HAWC/SWGO one usually organizes the entire analysis in bins of e.g. Core/Energy/Zenith, so what we have now is a code that generates a dataset for each of them and we get a SpectrumDatasetOnOff for each of them. Later they need to be stacked together in order to use the SensitivityEstimator
- HAWC/SWGO IRFs and other objects at this level of the analysis are usually RA/Dec sky maps in bins of energy, which in the case of gammapy translates into `MapDataset`s

During the first naïve reverse engineering experiments, the stacking of the `dataset_on_off` instances failed immediately because the code was asking for the exposures, and when using e.g. `gammapy.makers.utils.make_map_exposure_true_energy` the next issue is that everything in gammapy at this level requires *tables* (like `gammapy.irf.EffectiveAreaTable2D`) and not maps.

The third problem encountered is that for HAWC-like experiments there is no definition of *pointing* and thus no immediate definition of *offset* when referring to the format of these tables.

We recently managed to get an effective area in this format (in particular GADF v0.3) by defining the offset as the zenith distance between the Zenith and the instant position of the transiting source (so like CTA will provide IRFs at different zenith angles because it is effectively pointing there, we would do the same but from Zenith).

(If you think this use case is specific enough, we could open a dedicated issue instead of cramming all the discussion here, since you mention different things in your issue description.)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/gammapy/gammapy/issues/comments/1591360286/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/gammapy/gammapy/issues/4576/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/gammapy/gammapy/issues/4576/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/gammapy/gammapy/issues/comments/1591436780","html_url":"https://github.com/gammapy/gammapy/issues/4576#issuecomment-1591436780","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/gammapy/gammapy/issues/4576","id":1591436780,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAJm2KM5e22Xs","user":{"login":"HealthyPear","id":17836610,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3ODM2NjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17836610?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear","html_url":"https://github.com/HealthyPear","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-06-14T15:11:18Z","updated_at":"2023-06-14T15:11:18Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"> It seems to me that the `SensitivityEstimator` we have at the moment, is not the right tool in this context.
> 
> * If the data are not in the form of a `SpectrumDatasetOnOff`, it might not be worth trying to squeeze them into one.

The fact is that if we didn't have multiple (Map)Datasets, what is currently in gammapy should works for us. It's when we have to stack multiple of them that we get the problems. Right now we managed to get an `EffectiveAreaTable2D` with the caveats above, so we can try to make it work (I think we'll need to do the same with energy dispersion and PSF though).

For reference, the summarized sequence of operation we are trying is this one:
- we start from HAWC-like IRF files (so maps as I explain above) and an events file
- ``maker = MapDatasetMaker(selection=["exposure", "psf", "edisp"])```
- we build a list of datasets using `.get_observations` from a `DataStore`
- we define a pointing in the center of the FoV and a `CircleSkyRegion`
- we produce a `dataset_on_off` using `SpectrumDatasetOnOff.from_spectrum_dataset` from `dataset.to_spectrum_dataset(on_region)`
- we the *should* stack the `dataset_on_off` datasets together, but this doesn't work for the caveats I described above
- we produce a sensitivity table with `SensitivityEstimator` (this works with any single dataset, but of course it's not correct)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/gammapy/gammapy/issues/comments/1591436780/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

File ~/Applications/mambaforge/envs/astro/lib/python3.10/site-packages/astropy/units/decorators.py:316, in QuantityInput.__call__.<locals>.wrapper(*func_args, **func_kwargs)

```

caused by the deprecation and later removal of the keywords associated to plt.cga()

see https://matplotlib.org/stable/api/prev_api_changes/api_changes_3.4.0.html#pyplot-gca-and-figure-gca-keyword-arguments

The deprecation notice using matplotlib v3.4 read in fact as follows,

> Calling gca() with keyword arguments was deprecated in Matplotlib 3.4. 
> Starting two minor releases later, gca() will take no keyword arguments.  
> The gca() function should only be used to get the current axes,
> or if no axes exist, create new axes with default keyword arguments.
> To create a new axes with non-default arguments,
> use plt.axes() or plt.subplot().

So solution to this problem is to:
1. require a matpltlib version way more recent than the one you are currently using as the bottom limit in your `setup.cfg` (v1.4)
2. modifying the `plot_sky` function to do `ax = fig.add_subplot(projection='polar')` instead of `ax = plt.gca(projection='polar')`","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astroplan/issues/561/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astroplan/issues/561/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

Description

See title :)

### Expected behavior

Probably there is a good deep reason why this doesn't happen, but I would expect that a quantity initialized with an angle unit is effectively an angle. 

### How to Reproduce

```python
import astropy.units as u
from astropy.units import Quantity
from astropy.coordinates import Angle

rotation = 5 * u.deg
isinstance(rotation, Angle) # this returns False

angle = Angle("5d")
isinstance(angle, Quantity) # this returns True
```

### Versions

Usually, we write a script as,

```python
"""Module docstring"""

import bla

def main():

    # do stuff

if __name__ == "__main__":

    main()
```

I am using numpydoc via its Sphinx extension and I get

`XXX.main:GL08:The object does not have a docstring`

Technically, yes, it should have one, but it would be the same as the module dosctring in this case.

I don't know if there is any kind of convention about this particular case.

If there isn't, is there a way I can tell the checker to ignore e.g. the `main` function of a script?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpydoc/issues/455/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpydoc/issues/455/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpydoc/issues/244","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpydoc","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpydoc/issues/244/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpydoc/issues/244/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpydoc/issues/244/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/issues/244","id":517831171,"node_id":"MDU6SXNzdWU1MTc4MzExNzE=","number":244,"title":"RT02 enforces the pandas docstring guide, not the numpydoc docstring guide","user":{"login":"brandondavid","id":42078533,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQyMDc4NTMz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/42078533?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/brandondavid","html_url":"https://github.com/brandondavid","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brandondavid/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brandondavid/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brandondavid/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brandondavid/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brandondavid/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brandondavid/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brandondavid/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brandondavid/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brandondavid/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":7,"created_at":"2019-11-05T15:06:58Z","updated_at":"2023-03-14T14:48:22Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The [RT02 error](https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/blob/master/numpydoc/validate.py#L78) has the description:
> "The first line of the Returns section should contain only the type, unless multiple values are being returned"

This appears to enforce the [pandas docstring guide](https://dev.pandas.io/docs/development/contributing_docstring.html#section-4-returns-or-yields):
> "...no name will be provided, unless the method returns or yields more than one value"

In contrast, the [numpydoc docstring guide](https://numpydoc.readthedocs.io/en/latest/format.html#sections) states:
> "...the name of each return value is optional. The type of each return value is always required"

This is a concern for SciPy, where the general standard has been to specify the name of all return values, even when only a single value is returned.  As a very rough measure, over 42% of the functions in scipy.stats are currently tripping RT02 errors (and that's without bothering to bracket out the functions which return multiple values).

Possible solutions:
1) Update the numpydoc docstring guide to stipulate what RT02 enforces.  This would necessitate a lot of little edits (at least for SciPy) and the loss of some potentially valuable information from the documentation.

2) Remove RT02 from `numpydoc.validate`.  But then, pandas either loses that particular validation or has to (re)implement a version of the script in their project, which would be a reversal of the recent effort to bring things from pandas to numpydoc.

3) Or, as a compromise, demote RT02 to a warning, which would only report if specifically requested.  If numpydoc doesn't mind maintaining non-numpydoc warnings, a central collection would be convenient for numpydoc users looking for stricter validation.  Further, if there is a desire to update the numpydoc docstring guide, collaborating about such warnings would be a great place to start.
","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpydoc/issues/244/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpydoc/issues/244/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpydoc/issues/comments/1468241060","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/issues/244#issuecomment-1468241060","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpydoc/issues/244","id":1468241060,"node_id":"IC_kwDOALGa2c5Xg5Sk","user":{"login":"HealthyPear","id":17836610,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3ODM2NjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17836610?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear","html_url":"https://github.com/HealthyPear","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-03-14T14:48:21Z","updated_at":"2023-03-14T14:48:21Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"Hello,

not sure if this is the right place to ask for this, but in my code I have something like this,

```
"""
    Calculate the transit function in the cosine of the zenith angle.

    Parameters
    ----------
    czen : float
        Cosine of the zenith angle.
    dec : `~astropy.units.Quantity`
        Source declination in radians.
    latitude : `~astropy.coordinates.Angle`
        Observatory latitude.

    Returns
    -------
    dt_dcostheta : float
        Variation in time of the source escursion
        in the cosine of the zenith angle.
    """
```

and I get exactly the error subject of this issue,

```
WARNING: [numpydoc] Validation warnings while processing docstring for 'pyswgo.irfs.InstrumentResponseGenerator.transit_function_czen':
  RT02: The first line of the Returns section should contain only the type, unless multiple values are being returned
```

I don't really get what is triggering it...
Is it related to this issue or am I mistaken somewhere?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpydoc/issues/comments/1468241060/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpydoc/issues/456","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpydoc","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpydoc/issues/456/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpydoc/issues/456/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpydoc/issues/456/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/issues/456","id":1625112016,"node_id":"I_kwDOALGa2c5g3T3Q","number":456,"title":"Possibly unexpected warning for class attributes","user":{"login":"HealthyPear","id":17836610,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3ODM2NjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17836610?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear","html_url":"https://github.com/HealthyPear","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2023-03-15T09:32:48Z","updated_at":"2023-03-15T09:32:48Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I have a class like this,

```
class MyClass:
    """
    Summary line.

    Attributes
    ----------
    a: str
        Description of `a`.
    b: `.SomeType`
        Description of `b`.
    c: `.SomeOtherType`
        Description of `c`.
    """

    __slots__ = (
        "a",
        "b",
        "c",
    )

    def __init__(
        self,
        a: str,
        b: SomeType,
        c: SomeOtherType,
    ):

        if not isinstance(a, str):
            raise TypeError("`a` must be a str")

        if not isinstance(b, SomeType):
            raise TypeError("`b` must be an instance of  SomeType")

        if not isinstance(c, SomeOtherType):
            raise TypeError("`c` must be an instance of  SomeOtherType")

        self.a = a
        self.b = b
        self.c = c

    # methods after this line
```

I thought this was sufficient, but I still get warnings like this,

```
WARNING: [numpydoc] Validation warnings while processing docstring for 'XXX.MyClass.a':
  GL01: Docstring text (summary) should start in the line immediately after the opening quotes (not in the same line, or leaving a blank line in between)

    def __getattr__(self, attr):
        """
        Quantities are able to directly convert to other units that

        """
        if not self._include_easy_conversion_members:
            raise AttributeError(
                f"'{self.__class__.__name__}' object has no '{attr}' member"
            )

        def get_virtual_unit_attribute():
            registry = get_current_unit_registry().registry
            to_unit = registry.get(attr, None)
            if to_unit is None:
                return None

```

### Expected behavior

It should work the same in any environment if the package version is the same

### How to Reproduce

From a pdb session triggered by pytest I get that the triggering line is the following

```
-> lon[k] = sky_coords[k].geocentrictrueecliptic.lon.deg
(Pdb) k
0
(Pdb) sky_coords[k]
<SkyCoord (ICRS): (ra, dec) in deg
    (175., -0.5)>
```

indeed if (still inside pytest/pdb) I try to perform the transformation I get,

`(Pdb) sky_coords[k].geocentrictrueecliptic
*** AttributeError: Longitude instance has no attribute 'radian'`

To be sure I try to replicate the issue from scratch in a separate IPython session,

```ipython
In [1]: from astropy import units as u
   ...: from astropy.coordinates import SkyCoord
   ...: c = SkyCoord(ra=10.625*u.degree, dec=41.2*u.degree, frame='icrs')

In [2]: c.geocentrictrueecliptic
Out[2]:
<SkyCoord (GeocentricTrueEcliptic: equinox=J2000.000, obstime=J2000.000): (lon, lat, distance) in (deg, deg, )
    (27.76134106, 33.31188749, 1.)>

In [3]: c.geocentrictrueecliptic.lon.deg
Out[3]: 27.761341057229142
```

        def get_virtual_unit_attribute():
            registry = get_current_unit_registry().registry
            to_unit = registry.get(attr, None)

### Expected behavior

It should work the same in any environment if the package version is the same

### How to Reproduce

From a pdb session triggered by pytest I get that the triggering line is the following

```
-> lon[k] = sky_coords[k].geocentrictrueecliptic.lon.deg

0
(Pdb) sky_coords[k]
<SkyCoord (ICRS): (ra, dec) in deg
    (175., -0.5)>
```

indeed if (still inside pytest/pdb) I try to perform the transformation I get,

`(Pdb) sky_coords[k].geocentrictrueecliptic
*** AttributeError: Longitude instance has no attribute 'radian'`

To be sure I try to replicate the issue from scratch in a separate IPython session,

<SkyCoord (GeocentricTrueEcliptic: equinox=J2000.000, obstime=J2000.000): (lon, lat, distance) in (deg, deg, )
    (27.76134106, 33.31188749, 1.)>

In [3]: c.geocentrictrueecliptic.lon.deg
Out[3]: 27.761341057229142
```

    (27.76134106, 33.31188749, 1.)>

In [3]: c.geocentrictrueecliptic.lon.deg
Out[3]: 27.761341057229142
```

inconsistently","user":{"login":"HealthyPear","id":17836610,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3ODM2NjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17836610?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear","html_url":"https://github.com/HealthyPear","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":16191481,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNjE5MTQ4MQ==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy/labels/Bug","name":"Bug","color":"d93f0b","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2023-01-28T22:44:22Z","updated_at":"2023-01-28T22:44:22Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Description

Not sure if this is a feature request or a bug fix because in some cases is possible and in others not.
Also, apologies if somewhat it is possible, the docs do not have explicit examples of this.

Creating a QTable an adding/transforming units after its creation (like any Quantity) seems to be either not possible or unstable.

### Expected behavior

What I think would be cool is for QTables to have a `.to()` method that accepts a dictionary of units just like the `units` argument of the Table contructor to add previously inexistent units and transform to equivalent units (raising some unit error otherwise).

so we make a Qtable 

`t = QTable([a, b, c, d],names=('a', 'b', 'c', 'd')`

with e.g. a None, b in meters, c in seconds and d in eV

and we should be able to do this

`t.to({"a":"m", "c":"h"}`

with b and d unchanged,

but not this

`t.to({"a":"m", "c":"m"}`

which should raise a relevant exception.

### How to Reproduce

We can create a QTable like this, like the example on the docs shows,

```python
from astropy.table import QTable
import astropy.units as u
import numpy as np

a = np.array([1, 4, 5], dtype=np.int32)
b = [2.0, 5.0, 8.5]
c = ['x', 'y', 'z']
d = [10, 20, 30] * u.m / u.s

t = QTable([a, b, c, d],
           names=('a', 'b', 'c', 'd'),
           meta={'name': 'first table'})
```

and you can update the tables' units for the columns which do not have a unit

`t = QTable([a, b, c, d],names=('a', 'b', 'c', 'd'),units={"a":"m", "b":"s", "c":"m/s"})`

which by the way allows setting a unit to a column with string arguments, so this is should definitely not be possible to do!

But OK a and b now have a unit.

Though if I try to update the unit of "d", even only transforming to an equivalent one like "km/s" I cannot do it,

```
---------------------------------------------------------------------------

Cell In [19], line 1
----> 1 t = QTable([a, b, c, d],names=('a', 'b', 'c', 'd'),units={"a":"m", "b":"s", "c":"m/s", "d":"km/s"})

    882 if value not in (np.ma.masked, None):

[X ] I read [the conda-forge documentation](https://conda-forge.org/docs/user/introduction.html#how-can-i-install-packages-from-conda-forge) and could not find the solution for my problem there. Issue: I'm having problems compiling a program within conda that uses Geant4. I can reproduce the issue as follows: Within conda, I created a geant4 environment on both Linux CentOS 7 and Mac OS X Mojave: ``` conda create -y --name geant4 cmake geant4 ``` Then I try to build any of the examples using `cmake`: ``` cd $CONDA_PREFIX/share/Geant4-10.7.2/examples/basic/ mkdir B1-build cd B1-build cmake ../B1 ``` In Linux, this builds without a problem. But in Mac OS, `cmake` has a problem finding the EXPAT library. Here is the output:
``` cmake ../B1 -- The C compiler identification is AppleClang 11.0.0.11000033 -- The CXX compiler identification is AppleClang 11.0.0.11000033 -- Detecting C compiler ABI info -- Detecting C compiler ABI info - done -- Check for working C compiler: /Applications/Xcode.app/Contents/Developer/Toolchains/XcodeDefault.xctoolchain/usr/bin/cc - skipped -- Detecting C compile features -- Detecting C compile features - done -- Detecting CXX compiler ABI info -- Detecting CXX compiler ABI info - done -- Check for working CXX compiler: /Applications/Xcode.app/Contents/Developer/Toolchains/XcodeDefault.xctoolchain/usr/bin/c++ - skipped -- Detecting CXX compile features -- Detecting CXX compile features - done CMake Error at /Users/seligman/opt/anaconda3/envs/geant4/share/cmake-3.21/Modules/FindPackageHandleStandardArgs.cmake:230 (message): Could NOT find EXPAT: Found unsuitable version "2.0.1", but required is at least "2.2.5" (found /Users/seligman/opt/anaconda3/envs/grams-devel/lib/libexpat.dylib) Call Stack (most recent call first): /Users/seligman/opt/anaconda3/envs/geant4/share/cmake-3.21/Modules/FindPackageHandleStandardArgs.cmake:592 (_FPHSA_FAILURE_MESSAGE) /Users/seligman/opt/anaconda3/envs/geant4/share/cmake-3.21/Modules/FindEXPAT.cmake:65 (FIND_PACKAGE_HANDLE_STANDARD_ARGS) /Users/seligman/opt/anaconda3/envs/geant4/share/cmake-3.21/Modules/CMakeFindDependencyMacro.cmake:47 (find_package) /Users/seligman/opt/anaconda3/envs/geant4/lib/Geant4-10.7.2/Geant4Config.cmake:281 (find_dependency) CMakeLists.txt:16 (find_package) ```
As you can see in the details below, conda installed expat-2.4.1 as part of the geant4 installation. When I look at the library ` /Users/seligman/opt/anaconda3/envs/grams-devel/lib/libexpat.dylib`, I see it contains the string `expat_2.4.1`. Why is it that, using conda-forge-supplied cmake, expat, and geant4, and after finding a library that apparently is of the appropriate version, that cmake is reporting this error? And why only on Mac OS and not CentOS?
