[X ] I read [the conda-forge documentation](https://conda-forge.org/docs/user/introduction.html#how-can-i-install-packages-from-conda-forge) and could not find the solution for my problem there. Issue: I'm having problems compiling a program within conda that uses Geant4. I can reproduce the issue as follows: Within conda, I created a geant4 environment on both Linux CentOS 7 and Mac OS X Mojave: ``` conda create -y --name geant4 cmake geant4 ``` Then I try to build any of the examples using `cmake`: ``` cd $CONDA_PREFIX/share/Geant4-10.7.2/examples/basic/ mkdir B1-build cd B1-build cmake ../B1 ``` In Linux, this builds without a problem. But in Mac OS, `cmake` has a problem finding the EXPAT library. Here is the output:
``` cmake ../B1 -- The C compiler identification is AppleClang 11.0.0.11000033 -- The CXX compiler identification is AppleClang 11.0.0.11000033 -- Detecting C compiler ABI info -- Detecting C compiler ABI info - done -- Check for working C compiler: /Applications/Xcode.app/Contents/Developer/Toolchains/XcodeDefault.xctoolchain/usr/bin/cc - skipped -- Detecting C compile features -- Detecting C compile features - done -- Detecting CXX compiler ABI info -- Detecting CXX compiler ABI info - done -- Check for working CXX compiler: /Applications/Xcode.app/Contents/Developer/Toolchains/XcodeDefault.xctoolchain/usr/bin/c++ - skipped -- Detecting CXX compile features -- Detecting CXX compile features - done CMake Error at /Users/seligman/opt/anaconda3/envs/geant4/share/cmake-3.21/Modules/FindPackageHandleStandardArgs.cmake:230 (message): Could NOT find EXPAT: Found unsuitable version "2.0.1", but required is at least "2.2.5" (found /Users/seligman/opt/anaconda3/envs/grams-devel/lib/libexpat.dylib) Call Stack (most recent call first): /Users/seligman/opt/anaconda3/envs/geant4/share/cmake-3.21/Modules/FindPackageHandleStandardArgs.cmake:592 (_FPHSA_FAILURE_MESSAGE) /Users/seligman/opt/anaconda3/envs/geant4/share/cmake-3.21/Modules/FindEXPAT.cmake:65 (FIND_PACKAGE_HANDLE_STANDARD_ARGS) /Users/seligman/opt/anaconda3/envs/geant4/share/cmake-3.21/Modules/CMakeFindDependencyMacro.cmake:47 (find_package) /Users/seligman/opt/anaconda3/envs/geant4/lib/Geant4-10.7.2/Geant4Config.cmake:281 (find_dependency) CMakeLists.txt:16 (find_package) ```
As you can see in the details below, conda installed expat-2.4.1 as part of the geant4 installation. When I look at the library ` /Users/seligman/opt/anaconda3/envs/grams-devel/lib/libexpat.dylib`, I see it contains the string `expat_2.4.1`. Why is it that, using conda-forge-supplied cmake, expat, and geant4, and after finding a library that apparently is of the appropriate version, that cmake is reporting this error? And why only on Mac OS and not CentOS?

Description

Not sure if this is a feature request or a bug fix because in some cases is possible and in others not.
Also, apologies if somewhat it is possible, the docs do not have explicit examples of this.

Creating a QTable an adding/transforming units after its creation (like any Quantity) seems to be either not possible or unstable.

### Expected behavior

What I think would be cool is for QTables to have a `.to()` method that accepts a dictionary of units just like the `units` argument of the Table contructor to add previously inexistent units and transform to equivalent units (raising some unit error otherwise).

so we make a Qtable 

`t = QTable([a, b, c, d],names=('a', 'b', 'c', 'd')`

with e.g. a None, b in meters, c in seconds and d in eV

and we should be able to do this

`t.to({"a":"m", "c":"h"}`

with b and d unchanged,

but not this

`t.to({"a":"m", "c":"m"}`

which should raise a relevant exception.

### How to Reproduce

We can create a QTable like this, like the example on the docs shows,

```python
from astropy.table import QTable
import astropy.units as u
import numpy as np

a = np.array([1, 4, 5], dtype=np.int32)
b = [2.0, 5.0, 8.5]
c = ['x', 'y', 'z']
d = [10, 20, 30] * u.m / u.s

t = QTable([a, b, c, d],
           names=('a', 'b', 'c', 'd'),
           meta={'name': 'first table'})
```

and you can update the tables' units for the columns which do not have a unit

`t = QTable([a, b, c, d],names=('a', 'b', 'c', 'd'),units={"a":"m", "b":"s", "c":"m/s"})`

which by the way allows setting a unit to a column with string arguments, so this should definitely not be possible to do!

But OK a and b now have a unit.

Though if I try to update the unit of "d", even only transforming to an equivalent one like "km/s" I cannot do it,

```
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
AttributeError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In [19], line 1
----> 1 t = QTable([a, b, c, d],names=('a', 'b', 'c', 'd'),units={"a":"m", "b":"s", "c":"m/s", "d":"km/s"})

File ~/Applications/mambaforge/envs/astro/lib/python3.10/site-packages/astropy/table/table.py:852, in Table.__init__(self, data, masked, names, dtype, meta, copy, rows, copy_indices, units, descriptions, **kwargs)
    849 if self.masked not in (None, True, False):
    850     raise TypeError("masked property must be None, True or False")
--> 852 self._set_column_attribute('unit', units)
    853 self._set_column_attribute('description', descriptions)

File ~/Applications/mambaforge/envs/astro/lib/python3.10/site-packages/astropy/table/table.py:883, in Table._set_column_attribute(self, attr, values)
    880         value = None
    882 if value not in (np.ma.masked, None):
--> 883     setattr(self[name].info, attr, value)

File ~/Applications/mambaforge/envs/astro/lib/python3.10/site-packages/astropy/utils/data_info.py:231, in ParentAttribute.__set__(self, instance, value)
    227 if instance is None:
    228     # This is an unbound descriptor on the class
    229     raise ValueError('cannot set unbound descriptor')
--> 231 setattr(instance._parent, self.attr, value)

AttributeError: can't set attribute 'unit'
```


### Versions

macOS-11.7.3-x86_64-i386-64bit

So I have an in-memory QTable with column / units e.g. `a:m, b:None, c:eV` and before exporting the table to file I need to let the user to change e.g. _c_ from _eV_ to _GeV_, but as a developer I should do it in a way that doesn't need to know in advance  which columns should be converted.
Thus my proposal to use a dictionary which can be shorter than the number of columns - just like when initializing a Table with units (event though I start straight from a QTable)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy/issues/comments/1410887732/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

           meta={'name': 'first table'})
```

`t = QTable([a, b, c, d],names=('a', 'b', 'c', 'd'),units={"a":"m", "b":"s", "c":"m/s"})`

which by the way allows setting a unit to a column with string arguments, so this should definitely not be possible to do!

But OK a and b now have a unit.

r = QTable([a, b, c, d], names=('a', 'b', 'c', 'd'), units={"d":"m/s"})
```

If I join these mock tables it works,

```python
t = join(l, r, keys=["a"])
assert all(t["b"]==r["b"])
```

but in my case the order of the rows of the right table is all scrambled.

Unfortunately my code is not public for the moment also quite long and complex as its the first version, so I thought it might be easier to ask if someone has ever stumbled upon a similar issue and how did they debugged it.

For the moment I checked succesully that the single tables are as I expect and that the 2 common columns are equivalent and that their dtype is the same.

Checklist

- [X] I added a descriptive title
- [X] I searched open requests and couldn't find a duplicate

### What is the idea?

Define and install optional dependencies from an environment file (apologies if this is already possible, but I couldn't do it)

### Why is this needed?

It should resemble what is possible in setuptools

### What should happen?

A conda recipe defined by a file `environment.yaml` would look something like

```yaml
name : my_env
channels:
 - conda-forge
dependencies:
 - A
 - B
 - pip
 - pip:
    - G
optional_dependencies
    C:
      - C
    other:
      - D
      - E
      pip:
      - F
    seaborn:
      - seaborn
    all:
      - C
      - other
```

and then the installation command should accept a similar syntax to `pip`, namely

- `conda install -f environment.yaml` would install only (required) `dependencies`
- `conda install -f environment.yaml[C]` would install (required) `dependencies` + `C`
- `conda install -f environment.yaml[C, seaborn]` would install (required) `dependencies` + `C` + `seaborn`, but not `other`
- `conda install -f environment.yaml[all]` would install (required) `dependencies` + all optional depedencies

Not sure what would be the best syntax for the installation command, this is just an example.
Also, the `all` keyword might be also implicit. 

### Additional Context

Apologies if this is somewhat already possible, but I couldn't find a solution.

Is it possible to concatenate elements from arrays such as

`a = np.array([[],[], [1]],dtype=object)`

or concatenate more arrays like these,

```
b = np.empty(len(a), dtype=object)
c = np.array([[1],[], [0, 2, 3]],dtype=object)
```
?

The operation I am looking for should do e.g.

```
d = np.concatenate(a, b)
---> np.array([1],dtype=object)
```

or

```
d = np.concatenate(b, c)
---> np.array([1, 0, 2, 3],dtype=object)
```

or 

```
d = np.concatenate(a, b, c)
---> np.array([1, 1, 0, 2, 3],dtype=object)
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/22603/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/22603/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

### Description

This might be related to #13836, but I am not sure.

### Expected behavior

`u.Quantity(np.array([[1,2], [1,2,3], [1, 2]], dtype=object),u.m)`

should work and give back

`[[1, 2], [1, 2, 3], [1, 2]] m`

Similarly, I would expect that

`u.Quantity(np.array([[1,2], [], [1, 2]], dtype=object),u.m)`

would give

`[[1, 2], [], [1, 2]] m`

In case all elements are long the same it works already,

`u.Quantity(np.array([[1,2], [1,3], [1, 2]], dtype=object),u.m)`

gives

`[[1, 2], [1, 3], [1, 2]] m`

Apologies if this is somewhat already possible, but I couldn't find a solution.

Is it possible to concatenate elements from arrays such as

`a = np.array([[],[], [1]],dtype=object)`

or concatenate more arrays like these,

```
b = np.empty(len(a), dtype=object)
c = np.array([[1],[], [0, 2, 3]],dtype=object)
```
?

The operation I am looking for should do e.g.

```
d = np.concatenate(a, b)
---> np.array([1],dtype=object)
```

or

```
d = np.concatenate(b, c)
---> np.array([1, 0, 2, 3],dtype=object)
```

or 

```
d = np.concatenate(a, b, c)
---> np.array([1, 1, 0, 2, 3],dtype=object)
```

I tried with `numpy.ravel()`, but it doesn't do anything","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/22603/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/22603/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/1318499331","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/22603#issuecomment-1318499331","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/22603","id":1318499331,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP85OlrQD","user":{"login":"HealthyPear","id":17836610,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3ODM2NjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17836610?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear","html_url":"https://github.com/HealthyPear","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/HealthyPear/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-11-17T11:31:12Z","updated_at":"2022-11-17T11:31:12Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"Again, the concatenation is not the root problem for me (apart from when concatenating with array objects containing all empty elements, which doesn't work as I show above, but I can maybe remove from my dataset and concatenate mixed dtype objects).

Well, the story is long...

Basically, I have data read using Python3 as ragged arrays ([XCDF](https://github.com/jimbraun/XCDF/pull/93)) into [astropy QTables](https://docs.astropy.org/en/stable/api/astropy.table.QTable.html#astropy.table.QTable).

Unfortunately, astropy doesn't like dtype object arrays, even though they partially support it (which is the reason [I managed to get a table](https://github.com/astropy/astropy/issues/14014) with some units that I can at least take a look into - but I cannot really _use_, so I was trying to understand if I could make use of such column data a-posteriori).

At the same time, I think I might be better of with [Awkward Arrays](https://awkward-array.org/quickstart.html), but I have never used them, they for sure don't support units and it's a whole separate adventure (which is why I wanted to make sure here what was possible to do with this kind of arrays)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/1318576633/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

### Description
<!-- Provide a general description of the feature you would like. -->
<!-- If you want to, you can suggest a draft design or API. -->
<!-- This way we have a deeper discussion on the feature. -->

Not sure if this is already possible, but I tried looking around and it doesn't seem to me a clear feature in (pure) astropy.
I have only found code that uses WCS coordinates from existing data files or relatively complex code like the Fermi GBM tools.
Also the affiliated package "astropy regions" seems to be more complicated than what I would expect.

Is it possible to plot an area in the sky (like a rectangle or a circle or any area defined by angle ranges) using pure astropy without existing FITS file headers?

Suppose I work from an "aitoff" projection in equatorial coordinates.

I can plot single SkyCoord objects like the Crab Nebula or multiple ones based on some condition like the Galactic Plane.
What if I want to show, e.g.:
- a stripe of few degrees along the galactic plane
- a circle of fiew degrees around the Crab position
- the accessible FoV from an EarthLocation

Of course, I would expect this to work even if I transform from icrs to galactic and viceversa...

### Additional context
<!-- Add any other context or screenshots about the feature request here. -->
<!-- This part is optional. -->

I tried to do naïvely this

(Of course if there is nicer way to write this code I am all ears.

Thank you in advance)

```python
from astropy import units as u
from astropy.coordinates import SkyCoord
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.patches import Rectangle


# Define sources
vernal_point = SkyCoord(ra=0*u.degree, dec=0*u.degree, frame='icrs')
ecliptic = SkyCoord(lon=np.arange(-180,180), lat=0*u.deg, unit=u.deg, frame='barycentricmeanecliptic')
galactic_plane = SkyCoord(l=np.arange(-180,180), b=0, unit=u.deg, frame="galactic")
celestial_equator = SkyCoord(ra=np.arange(-180,180)*u.deg, dec=0*u.deg, frame='icrs')
crab = SkyCoord.from_name("Crab Nebula")

plt.figure(figsize=(8,6))
plt.subplot(111, projection="aitoff")
ax=plt.gca()
plt.grid(True)

plot_options = {"marker":"o"}

def plot_sky_coordinates(skycoord,frame="icrs",**kwargs):
    
    c = skycoord.transform_to(frame)
    if frame=="galactic":
        x=c.l
        y=c.b
    elif frame=="icrs":
        x=c.ra
        y=c.dec
    else:
        raise ValueError("Only supported coordinate frames are 'icrs' and 'galactic'")
    plt.plot(-x.wrap_at(180 * u.deg).radian, y.radian, **kwargs)


def fill_patch(origin, frame="icrs", ax=None, height=None, width=None, shape="rectangle", **kwargs):
    
    c = SkyCoord(origin[0], origin[1], unit=u.deg).transform_to(frame)
    
    if frame=="galactic":
        x=c.l
        y=c.b
    elif frame=="icrs":
        x=c.ra
        y=c.dec
    else:
        raise ValueError("Only supported coordinate frames are 'icrs' and 'galactic'")
        
    o = (x.wrap_at(180 * u.deg).radian,y.radian)
    
    if shape=="rectangle":
        
        p = Rectangle(o,
                          (width*u.deg).to_value("rad"),
                          (height*u.deg).to_value("rad"), angle=0.0, alpha=0.5)
    ax.add_patch(p)

    
frame = "icrs"

plot_sky_coordinates(vernal_point,**plot_options, label="Vernal point", ls="", frame=frame)
plot_sky_coordinates(crab,label="Crab Nebula", ls="",marker="*", frame=frame)
plot_sky_coordinates(celestial_equator,label="Celestial equator", ls="dashed", frame=frame)
plot_sky_coordinates(ecliptic,label="Ecliptic", ls="dashed", frame=frame)
plot_sky_coordinates(galactic_plane,label="Galactic plane", ls="dashed", frame=frame)

fill_patch((-180,-60), frame=frame, ax=ax, height=120, width=360, shape="rectangle", alpha=0.2, label="patch")

if frame=="galactic":
    plt.suptitle("Galactic coordinates")
    plt.xlabel("Galactic longitude (deg)")
    plt.ylabel("Galactic latitude (deg)")
elif frame in ["icrs", "fk5"]:
    plt.suptitle("Equatorial coordinates")
    plt.xlabel("Right Ascension (deg)")
    plt.ylabel("Declination (deg)")
plt.subplots_adjust(top=1.2,bottom=0.0)
plt.legend(loc='best', bbox_to_anchor=(0.75, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5))
plt.show()
```

Also the affiliated package "astropy regions" seems to be more complicated than what I would expect.

I can plot single SkyCoord objects like the Crab Nebula or multiple ones based on some condition like the Galactic Plane.

- a stripe of few degrees along the galactic plane
- a circle of fiew degrees around the Crab position
- the accessible FoV from an EarthLocation

Of course, I would expect this to work even if I transform from icrs to galactic and viceversa...

### Description
<!-- Provide a general description of the feature you would like. -->
<!-- If you want to, you can suggest a draft design or API. -->
<!-- This way we have a deeper discussion on the feature. -->

Not sure if this is already possible, but I tried looking around and it doesn't seem to me a clear feature in (pure) astropy.
I have only found code that uses WCS coordinates from existing data files or relatively complex code like the Fermi GBM tools.
Also the affiliated package "astropy regions" seems to be more complicated than what I would expect.

Is it possible to plot an area in the sky (like a rectangle or a circle or any area defined by angle ranges) using pure astropy without existing FITS file headers?

Suppose I work from an "aitoff" projection in equatorial coordinates.

I can plot single SkyCoord objects like the Crab Nebula or multiple ones based on some condition like the Galactic Plane.
What if I want to show, e.g.:
- a stripe of few degrees along the galactic plane
- a circle of fiew degrees around the Crab position
- the accessible FoV from an EarthLocation

Of course, I would expect this to work even if I transform from icrs to galactic and viceversa...

### Additional context
<!-- Add any other context or screenshots about the feature request here. -->
<!-- This part is optional. -->

I tried to do naïvely this

(Of course if there is nicer way to write this code I am all ears.

Thank you in advance)

```python
from astropy import units as u
from astropy.coordinates import SkyCoord
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.patches import Rectangle


# Define sources
vernal_point = SkyCoord(ra=0*u.degree, dec=0*u.degree, frame='icrs')
ecliptic = SkyCoord(lon=np.arange(-180,180), lat=0*u.deg, unit=u.deg, frame='barycentricmeanecliptic')
galactic_plane = SkyCoord(l=np.arange(-180,180), b=0, unit=u.deg, frame="galactic")
celestial_equator = SkyCoord(ra=np.arange(-180,180)*u.deg, dec=0*u.deg, frame='icrs')
crab = SkyCoord.from_name("Crab Nebula")

plt.figure(figsize=(8,6))
plt.subplot(111, projection="aitoff")
ax=plt.gca()
plt.grid(True)

plot_options = {"marker":"o"}

def plot_sky_coordinates(skycoord,frame="icrs",**kwargs):
    
    c = skycoord.transform_to(frame)
    if frame=="galactic":
        x=c.l
        y=c.b
    elif frame=="icrs":
        x=c.ra
        y=c.dec
    else:
        raise ValueError("Only supported coordinate frames are 'icrs' and 'galactic'")
    plt.plot(-x.wrap_at(180 * u.deg).radian, y.radian, **kwargs)


def fill_patch(origin, frame="icrs", ax=None, height=None, width=None, shape="rectangle", **kwargs):
    
    c = SkyCoord(origin[0], origin[1], unit=u.deg).transform_to(frame)
    
    if frame=="galactic":
        x=c.l
        y=c.b
    elif frame=="icrs":
        x=c.ra
        y=c.dec
    else:
        raise ValueError("Only supported coordinate frames are 'icrs' and 'galactic'")
        
    o = (x.wrap_at(180 * u.deg).radian,y.radian)
    
    if shape=="rectangle":
        
        p = Rectangle(o,
                          (width*u.deg).to_value("rad"),
                          (height*u.deg).to_value("rad"), angle=0.0, alpha=0.5)
    ax.add_patch(p)

    
frame = "icrs"

plot_sky_coordinates(vernal_point,**plot_options, label="Vernal point", ls="", frame=frame)
plot_sky_coordinates(crab,label="Crab Nebula", ls="",marker="*", frame=frame)
plot_sky_coordinates(celestial_equator,label="Celestial equator", ls="dashed", frame=frame)
plot_sky_coordinates(ecliptic,label="Ecliptic", ls="dashed", frame=frame)
plot_sky_coordinates(galactic_plane,label="Galactic plane", ls="dashed", frame=frame)

fill_patch((-180,-60), frame=frame, ax=ax, height=120, width=360, shape="rectangle", alpha=0.2, label="patch")

if frame=="galactic":
    plt.suptitle("Galactic coordinates")
    plt.xlabel("Galactic longitude (deg)")
    plt.ylabel("Galactic latitude (deg)")
elif frame in ["icrs", "fk5"]:
    plt.suptitle("Equatorial coordinates")
    plt.xlabel("Right Ascension (deg)")
    plt.ylabel("Declination (deg)")
plt.subplots_adjust(top=1.2,bottom=0.0)
plt.legend(loc='best', bbox_to_anchor=(0.75, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5))
plt.show()
```

### Description
Not sure if this is a bug or expected behaviour, but it's for sure unexpected to me...

When diffing times the scale and format change

times = [Time("2017-01-01T00:00:00.000", scale='utc', format='isot'),
 Time("2017-01-01T00:02:00.000", scale='utc', format='isot')]

### Expected behavior
<!-- What did you expect to happen. -->

The resulting TimeDelta object should maintain scale and format of the original Time array (or list of Times) and return time in seconds since I start from UTC

### Actual behavior
<!-- What actually happened. -->
<!-- Was the output confusing or poorly described? -->

Given `scale='utc', format='isot'` I get `scale='tai' format='jd'` resulting of course in a different time difference value

### Steps to Reproduce
<!-- Ideally a code example could be provided so we can run it ourselves. -->
<!-- If you are pasting code, use triple backticks (```) around
your code snippet. -->
<!-- If necessary, sanitize your screen output to be pasted so you do not
reveal secrets like tokens and passwords. -->



```python
from math import ceil

import astropy.units as u
import astropy.Time as Time
import numpy as np

total_time=24*u.h
time_step=1*u.min
time_samples = ceil(total_time.to_value(time_step))
start_time=Time.now()
start_time.format="iso"
obstime = start_time + np.linspace(0, 24, time_samples) * u.h
print(obstime.format)
print(obstime.scale)
print(np.diff(obstime[:2]))
```

results in

```pycon
iso
utc
[<TimeDelta object: scale='tai' format='jd' value=0.000694927032661552>]
```

### Description
Not sure if this is a bug or expected behaviour, but it's for sure unexpected to me...

When diffing times the scale and format change.

### Expected behavior
<!-- What did you expect to happen. -->

The resulting TimeDelta object should maintain scale and format of the original Time array (or list of Times) and return time in seconds since I start from UTC

### Actual behavior
<!-- What actually happened. -->
<!-- Was the output confusing or poorly described? -->

Given `scale='utc', format='isot'` I get `scale='tai' format='jd'` resulting of course in a different time difference value

### Steps to Reproduce
<!-- Ideally a code example could be provided so we can run it ourselves. -->
<!-- If you are pasting code, use triple backticks (```) around
your code snippet. -->
<!-- If necessary, sanitize your screen output to be pasted so you do not
reveal secrets like tokens and passwords. -->



```python
from math import ceil

import astropy.units as u
import astropy.Time as Time
import numpy as np

total_time=24*u.h
time_step=1*u.min
time_samples = ceil(total_time.to_value(time_step))
start_time=Time.now()
start_time.format="iso"
obstime = start_time + np.linspace(0, 24, time_samples) * u.h
print(obstime.format)
print(obstime.scale)
print(np.diff(obstime[:2]))
```

results in

```pycon
iso
utc
[<TimeDelta object: scale='tai' format='jd' value=0.000694927032661552>]
```

Plot directive clutters the output directory with unneeded files","user":{"login":"MikhailRyazanov","id":12854133,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEyODU0MTMz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/12854133?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/MikhailRyazanov","html_url":"https://github.com/MikhailRyazanov","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MikhailRyazanov/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MikhailRyazanov/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MikhailRyazanov/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MikhailRyazanov/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MikhailRyazanov/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MikhailRyazanov/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MikhailRyazanov/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MikhailRyazanov/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MikhailRyazanov/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2022-09-25T19:16:05Z","updated_at":"2022-10-28T20:07:54Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Bug summary Using the Matplotlib plot directive in Sphinx documentation results in cluttering the output directory with all the produced image files in all generated formats even when they are not needed (``plot_html_show_formats = False``). This is especially obvious with the ``singlehtml`` builder, where all the necessary images must be inside the ``_images`` subdirectory, but in fact, in addition to ``_images``, the whole directory tree with every image in every format is copied to the output directory, although nothing of it is referenced from the HTML file. What is more interesting, if another builder is executed first, then its cached images are reused properly — only the necessary files are copied into ``_images``, without cluttering the output directory with unneeded files and subdirectories. ### Code for reproduction See a minimal working example at https://github.com/MikhailRyazanov/plot-clutter. ### Actual outcome From step 1 of the example above: ``` $ tree build/singlehtml build/singlehtml ├── _images │   ├── index-1.svg │   ├── other-1.svg │   └── script.svg ├── index-1.pdf ├── index-1.svg ├── index.html ├── objects.inv ├── _static │   └── subdir ├── other-1.pdf ├── other-1.svg ├── script.pdf └── script.svg ``` ### Expected outcome From step 2 of the example above: ``` build/singlehtml ├── _images │   ├── index-1.svg │   ├── other-1.svg │   └── script.svg ├── index.html ├── objects.inv └── _static ``` ### Additional information In practice, this undesired behavior directly affects the downloadable documentation at {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/24005","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/24005/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/24005/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/24005/events","html_url":"https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/24005","id":1385133679,"node_id":"I_kwDOABUios5Sj3Zv","number":24005,"title":"[Bug]: Plot directive clutters the output directory with unneeded files","user":{"login":"MikhailRyazanov","id":12854133,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEyODU0MTMz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/12854133?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/MikhailRyazanov","html_url":"https://github.com/MikhailRyazanov","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MikhailRyazanov/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MikhailRyazanov/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MikhailRyazanov/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MikhailRyazanov/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MikhailRyazanov/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MikhailRyazanov/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MikhailRyazanov/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MikhailRyazanov/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MikhailRyazanov/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":6,"created_at":"2022-09-25T19:16:05Z","updated_at":"2022-10-28T20:08:58Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Bug summary

Using the Matplotlib plot directive in Sphinx documentation results in cluttering the output directory with all the produced image files in all generated formats even when they are not needed (``plot_html_show_formats = False``). This is especially obvious with the ``singlehtml`` builder, where all the necessary images must be inside the ``_images`` subdirectory, but in fact, in addition to ``_images``, the whole directory tree with every image in every format is copied to the output directory, although nothing of it is referenced from the HTML file.

What is more interesting, if another builder is executed first, then its cached images are reused properly — only the necessary files are copied into ``_images``, without cluttering the output directory with unneeded files and subdirectories.


### Code for reproduction

See a minimal working example at https://github.com/MikhailRyazanov/plot-clutter.


### Actual outcome

From step 1 of the example above:
```
$ tree build/singlehtml
build/singlehtml
├── _images
│   ├── index-1.svg
│   ├── other-1.svg
│   └── script.svg
├── index-1.pdf
├── index-1.svg
├── index.html
├── objects.inv
├── _static
│   <skipped>
└── subdir
    ├── other-1.pdf
    ├── other-1.svg
    ├── script.pdf
    └── script.svg
```

### Expected outcome

From step 2 of the example above:
```
build/singlehtml
├── _images
│   ├── index-1.svg
│   ├── other-1.svg
│   └── script.svg
├── index.html
├── objects.inv
└── _static
    <skipped>
```

### Additional information

In practice, this undesired behavior directly affects the downloadable documentation at Read the docs — see [issue #9068 there](https://github.com/readthedocs/readthedocs.org/issues/9068).

My PR https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/pull/24205 correctly treats the second part — it does copy all the necessary files into docs/build/html/_downloads, so they will be available to your CI (for the final web page, you really need to expose only docs/build/html, not the whole docs/build) and be linked correctly from the HTML files.

Ok good.

> It, however, doesn't modify the caching part, so you'll still see docs/examples generated. This is a separate problem, and I'm not sure that Matplotlib maintainers would be willing to do anything about it because they might just claim that plotting scripts must be within the source tree (not its grandparent, as in your case). Nevertheless, a simple solution that I can propose is to make a [symlink](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Symbolic_link) directory docs/examples pointing to ../../examples and accordingly change

I think there is an easier cross-platform solution for this part, since the `docs/examples` folder would be just for temporarely caching:
- you add the created directory to .`gitignore` (just to be sure to not clutter the project repo)
- you update the Makefile to remove that folder at the end of the build when the HTML material is safe and sound.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/comments/1296277531/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

[ x] I read [the conda-forge documentation](https://conda-forge.org/docs/user/introduction.html#how-can-i-install-packages-from-conda-forge) and could not find the solution for my problem there. I followed the instructions on installing and building the geant4 example posted in #4 but running ./exampleB1 in interactive mode I get the following output and the process is stuck: ``` $./exampleB1 Available UI session types: [ Qt, GAG, tcsh, csh ] G4WT0 > /control/saveHistory G4WT1 > /control/saveHistory G4WT0 > /run/verbose 2 G4WT1 > /run/verbose 2 G4WT0 > /run/initialize G4WT0 > /run/physicsModified G4WT1 > /run/initialize G4WT1 > /run/physicsModified qt.qpa.window: > has active key-value observers (KVO)! These will stop working now that the window is recreated, and will result in exceptions when the observers are removed. Break in QCocoaWindow::recreateWindowIfNeeded to debug. ``` In the geant4 forum this warning is discussed, but they do not seem to use Conda: [https://geant4-forum.web.cern.ch/t/qwidget-warning-while-running-exampeb1-in-mac-big-sur/3975](url) There it is also stated that geant4 only supports macOS 11.2 from geant4 version 10.7 on. Does this relate to my issue, and could I just wait for geant4 10.7 to be on conda-forge, relating to #18 ?

Comment:

Hi all,

I am trying to build a CMake-based project within a conda environment on macos in which HEALPix is one of the required dependencies.

I am not at all an expert of CMake, so I just went around the `src` folder of the project I am trying to build and found `vim ../src/cmake/Packages/HEALPix.cmake`.

In this file (which I think it's not produced at build time, it should be really how the developers expect the package to be configured, am I right?) I can see the following (I just show the beginning, let me know if you need to see more),

```
################################################################################
  2 # Module to find HEALPix-cxx                                                   #
  3 #                                                                              #
  4 #   HEALPIX_FOUND                                                              #
  5 #   HEALPIX_LIBRARIES                                                          #
  6 #   HEALPIX_INCLUDE_DIR                                                        #
  7 #   HEALPIX_BIN_DIR                                                            #
  8 #   HEALPIX_LIB_DIR                                                            #
  9 #   HEALPIX_CPPFLAGS                                                           #
 10 #   HEALPIX_LDFLAGS                                                            #
 11 ################################################################################
 12
 13 SET (HEALPix_FIND_QUIETLY TRUE)
 14 SET (HEALPix_FIND_REQUIRED FALSE)
 15
 16 IF (NOT HEALPIX_FOUND)
 17
 18   # Search user environment for healpix-cxx config script; then default paths
 19   FIND_PATH (HEALPIX_BIN_DIR healpix-cxx-config PATHS $ENV{HEALPIXROOT}/bin
 20     NO_DEFAULT_PATH)
 21   FIND_PATH (HEALPIX_BIN_DIR healpix-cxx-config)
```

On an Ubuntu machine, this package is built from source.
`HEALPIXROOT` is defined and under it the bin directory hosts the healpix-cxx-config executable.

On my macos (but I think it's general), conda places everything under `$CONDA_PREFIX/bin` where I cannot find such configurable.

Another difference is that on macos I cam trying to use healpix-cxx 3.80, while the ubuntu machine used 3.31.

Is this executable supposed to be there in my case?
Conda should already place all the headers and libraries in the default places, and I can see from the Cmake configuration that things like  FFTW3, CFITSIO, GSL, etc are found without having to do anything else, but HEALPIX it's not found even at the beginning.
